name: Big-O complexity
description: Asymptotic complexity classes and the typical time complexity of common operations and algorithms — binary search, quicksort, hash lookup, sorting, graph traversal. Term: an operation or algorithm; definition: its Big-O plus a short note. Quick recall cards for interviews and day-to-day reasoning.
primer:
**Big-O** describes how an algorithm's work grows as the input size $n$ grows — it keeps only the dominant term and drops constants, so it captures the *shape* of the scaling, not the exact count.

## The complexity classes (best to worst)

From fastest-growing to slowest, for large $n$: $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n\log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n)$.

- **$O(1)$ constant** — work is independent of $n$. Array index, stack push, average hash lookup.
- **$O(\log n)$ logarithmic** — each step discards a *constant fraction* of what's left, so the step count grows like the log. Binary search halves the range each step.
- **$O(n)$ linear** — one pass; you touch each element a constant number of times. A scan to find the max.
- **$O(n\log n)$ linearithmic** — the proven *lower bound for comparison-based sorting*. Merge sort, heap sort, average quicksort.
- **$O(n^2)$ quadratic** — typically a pair of nested loops over the input, or comparing every pair. Bubble/insertion sort.
- **$O(2^n)$ exponential** — work *doubles* with each added element; intractable beyond small $n$. Enumerating all subsets, brute force.

## Why classes matter

For large $n$ the class dominates everything: an $O(\log n)$ algorithm beats $O(n)$, which beats $O(n^2)$, regardless of constant factors. **Average vs worst case** can differ — quicksort is $O(n\log n)$ average but $O(n^2)$ worst (bad pivot on sorted data); a hash map is $O(1)$ average but $O(n)$ worst (everything collides into one bucket).

## Search & sort

- **Binary search** — $O(\log n)$, but *requires sorted data*; on unsorted data you fall back to a linear scan.
- **Linear search** — $O(n)$, works on unsorted data, no preprocessing.
- **Quicksort** — $O(n\log n)$ average, $O(n^2)$ worst; in-place and cache-friendly, fast in practice.
- **Merge sort** — $O(n\log n)$ in *all* cases but needs $O(n)$ extra space; *stable* (quicksort is not).
- **Bubble / insertion sort** — $O(n^2)$; insertion is $O(n)$ on nearly-sorted input.

## Structure operations

- **Hash-map lookup/insert** — $O(1)$ average (good hash, low load), $O(n)$ worst (collisions).
- **Array access by index** — $O(1)$ (contiguous memory, address computed directly); middle insert is $O(n)$ (shifting).
- **Linked-list search** — $O(n)$ (no random access, follow pointers); insert at a known node is $O(1)$.
- **Balanced BST** (red-black, AVL) — $O(\log n)$ search/insert/delete; height kept $\propto \log n$.
- **Binary heap** — $O(\log n)$ insert / extract-min (sift up/down a tree of height $\log n$); $O(1)$ peek root.
- **Graph BFS / DFS** — $O(V+E)$ with an adjacency list: each vertex and edge visited once.

cards.definition: $O(1)$. The work done does not depend on the input size $n$. Example: indexing into an array, pushing onto a stack, or a hash lookup on average.
$O(\log n)$. Each step discards a constant fraction of the remaining input, so the count of steps grows like the logarithm of $n$. Example: binary search on a sorted array.
cards.summary: $O(\log n)$ — halves the problem each step.

cards.definition: $O(n)$. Work grows in direct proportion to the input size — you touch each element a constant number of times. Example: a single scan of an array to find the maximum.
$O(n\log n)$. The lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Example: merge sort, heap sort, and average-case quicksort.
$O(n^2)$. Typically arises from a pair of nested loops over the input. Example: bubble sort, insertion sort, or comparing every pair of elements.
$O(2^n)$. Work doubles with each extra element; intractable beyond small $n$. Example: naive recursive enumeration of all subsets, or brute-forcing a combination.
$O(\log n)$. Repeatedly halves a sorted array to locate a target. Requires the data to be sorted first; on unsorted data you would need a linear scan instead.
$O(n)$. Scans elements one by one until the target is found or the end is reached. Works on unsorted data and needs no preprocessing.
$O(n\log n)$ on average, but $O(n^2)$ in the worst case (e.g. an already-sorted array with poor pivot choice). In-place and cache-friendly, which makes it fast in practice.
$O(n\log n)$ in all cases (best, average, worst), but needs $O(n)$ extra space for merging. It is stable, unlike quicksort.
$O(n^2)$ average and worst case. Insertion sort is $O(n)$ on nearly-sorted input, which makes it useful for small or almost-ordered arrays.
$O(1)$ on average, assuming a good hash function and a low load factor. Worst case is $O(n)$ if many keys collide into one bucket.
$O(1)$. Elements sit in contiguous memory, so the address is computed directly from the index. Inserting in the middle, however, is $O(n)$ because elements must shift.
$O(n)$. There is no random access — you must follow pointers from the head. Insertion at a known node is $O(1)$, which is the trade-off versus arrays.
$O(\log n)$. A self-balancing tree (red-black, AVL) keeps height proportional to $\log n$, so search, insert and delete stay logarithmic even in the worst case.
$O(\log n)$. A binary heap sifts an element up or down a tree of height $\log n$. Peeking at the min/max root is $O(1)$.
$O(V + E)$ — vertices plus edges. Each vertex and each edge is visited once when the graph is stored as an adjacency list.
